method: Transcorrelated (TC) correction for He at Level 3
result: NEGATIVE — adiabatic separation incompatible with TC
root_cause: The TC operator G = -d/dr_12 decomposes into angular G_ang (R-independent, O(1)) and radial G_R = -(sqrt(u)/2)*d/dR. The standard V_ee = 1/(R*sqrt(u)) enters the angular eigenvalue as R*C_ee, which is O(R). Removing V_ee and replacing with G_ang gives angular eigenvalues that are O(R) too negative. The G_R term in the radial equation cannot compensate because it is a first-order derivative (modifies the radial kinetic energy), not a potential term. The net effect is ~45-47% error. The TC method requires a variational or FCI framework where H_TC is constructed and diagonalized directly, not an adiabatic separation.

approaches_tested.name: Direct TC angular replacement
approaches_tested.description: Replace V_ee in angular Hamiltonian with G_tilde_ang, use eig for non-Hermitian

approaches_tested.name: Perturbative TC correction
approaches_tested.description: Solve standard angular problem, compute TC correction as Delta_V_eff

approaches_tested.name: Full TC with G_R in radial equation
approaches_tested.description: TC angular eigenvalues + first-derivative G_R coupling in radial equation
approaches_tested.result: ~45% error — G_R helps ~2% but angular eigenvalue still wrong by O(R)

key_observations: V_ee contributes O(R) to angular eigenvalue; G_ang contributes O(1)
G_R = -(sqrt(u)/2)*d/dR is a first-order radial coupling, not a potential
Including G_R helps ~2% but cannot compensate for missing V_ee repulsion
Large imaginary eigenvalues (up to 1.04) at l_max >= 1 from non-Hermitian TC
TC convergence with l_max is negligible (45.78% -> 45.43%) — not the expected exponential
The adiabatic separation fundamentally requires V_ee to be a multiplicative operator
conclusion: The TC Jastrow approach is structurally incompatible with the adiabatic hyperspherical method. The adiabatic separation exploits the multiplicative nature of V_ee to include it entirely in the angular eigenvalue problem. The TC transformation replaces this multiplicative operator with a first-order differential operator that couples angular and radial degrees of freedom, breaking the clean adiabatic separation. This is analogous to how the cusp (Track U-Y) is an embedding exchange constant — the TC handles the cusp by removing V_ee, but this removal is incompatible with the adiabatic framework. The Schwartz post-correction (Track X) remains the correct approach for the adiabatic solver.
